lens.included_activities: The industry activity assessed for task and demand context is government firefighting, fire prevention, and combined fire-rescue or ambulance response; under the fixed LMM outsourced-service lens, no establishment qualifies.
lens.excluded_activities: Commercial firefighting, forest firefighting, private fire-alarm and sprinkler services, ambulance-only providers, government agencies without transferable equity, volunteer associations that are not transferable operating firms, and all entities outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Government departments deliver tax- and appropriation-funded public safety rather than recurring services to arm's-length customers for commercial revenue; commercial firefighting is classified outside 922160.

a.bridge: BLS measures firefighters across government employers rather than a wage-weighted 922160 task census. I map incident-report drafting, pre-incident planning, inspection and prevention documentation, training administration, resource analysis, scheduling, inventory records, and alert triage as exposed; emergency driving, suppression, rescue, medical treatment, equipment maintenance, drills, and scene command remain physical or high-consequence. The firefighter proxy may understate administrative and prevention staff while overstating exposure if reports require extensive human verification.
a.rationale: Modern data tools can reduce documentation and improve planning, but the dominant work is embodied emergency response under severe safety and liability constraints.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task diaries from career, combination, and volunteer departments, paired with computer-aided dispatch, records-management, inspection, and NERIS usage logs, would materially revise the estimate.
caveats: No compensation-to-receipts ratio exists because this is a government code.
Occupation data do not isolate 922160 or prevention and administrative staff.

rho.rationale: Cloud incident reporting, data integration, drafting, scheduling, preplanning, and local risk analytics can be implemented within five years. Procurement cycles, fragmented local systems, cybersecurity, sensitive medical data, interoperability, public records, union and civil-service rules, false alerts, liability, and limited volunteer-department capacity constrain realization.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments across departments measuring report time, data completeness, dispatch accuracy, inspection throughput, false alerts, training time, cybersecurity incidents, and sustained user adoption would move the range.
caveats: Bounded judgment rather than an observed implementation rate.
NERIS demonstrates national data modernization, not substitution of emergency-response labor.

e.rationale: The 2022 NAICS manual defines 922160 as government establishments and explicitly classifies commercial firefighting in 561990. Government departments do not meet the fixed population of transferable LMM firms with normalized EBITDA.
e.change_evidence: A future NAICS revision that moves commercial contract fire-protection firms into 922160, or evidence that the dataset includes private transferable entities despite the official definition, would change this value.
caveats: Zero reflects an observed classification boundary, not an imputation for missing data.
Private contractors may perform adjacent work, but they belong to other NAICS codes.

s5.rationale: There are no eligible firms under the fixed lens, so a probability of qualifying control transfer is undefined rather than zero.
s5.change_evidence: Evidence of private transferable firms correctly classified in 922160, followed by denominator-based ownership and transaction records, would be required to estimate this primitive.
caveats: Government reorganizations, elected control changes, mutual-aid agreements, and privatization contracts are not qualifying equity control transfers.
The supplied LMM firm count is missing.

q.rationale: The code has no eligible commercial operator-customer revenue model under which implemented gross benefits could be retained after pricing and pass-through.
q.change_evidence: A verified population of private 922160 firms with customer contracts, renewal pricing, appropriations, and benefit-sharing terms would be required to estimate retention.
caveats: Budget savings and public-service productivity are economically meaningful but are not commercial operator retention as defined.
Commercial firefighting is classified outside this code.

d5.bridge: BLS projects firefighter employment to grow 3 percent from 2024 to 2034, and USFA reports 31,867,167 fire-department incidents in 2023, of which 66.2 percent were rescue and EMS calls. I translate occupational growth and current all-hazards workload into modest five-year growth in constant-quality public fire-protection demand, with upside from population aging, EMS intensity, climate and disaster response and downside from prevention, false-alarm reduction, fiscal limits, and shifting ambulance responsibility. Employment may overstate quantity if staffing standards rise and understate it if productivity improves; reported incidents omit nonreporting departments and do not provide a five-year forecast.
d5.rationale: Emergency medical and all-hazards response make demand broader than structural fires, while public budgets and prevention keep central growth moderate despite volatile disaster exposure.
d5.change_evidence: Comparable NERIS incident cohorts, population and age projections by service area, response-time standards, insurance loss data, local staffing plans, and five-year municipal capital and operating budgets would materially revise the range.
caveats: Employment is not a direct service-quantity measure.
Wildfire activity is highly volatile and most forest firefighting is classified outside 922160.

o.rationale: Nearly all year-five quantity still requires an accountable public fire or rescue operator to accept dispatch, make life-safety decisions, command scenes, enter hazardous environments, treat patients, maintain readiness, and document legal responsibility. Prevention technology and automatic systems can avert or resolve a small share of calls, but software cannot own emergency authority or physical response.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of widespread autonomous suppression, remote medical response replacing apparatus dispatch, commercial substitution of municipal departments, or large verified reductions in operator-attended incidents would move the estimate.
caveats: The accountable operator is a government agency, not an eligible LMM firm.
This primitive is a demand characteristic and does not override zero firm eligibility.

narrative.executive_view: The code is structurally outside the commercial roll-up lens: Census defines it as government fire-protection establishments and assigns commercial firefighting elsewhere. AI can improve public-department data and administration, but there is no qualifying firm population or commercial benefit-retention construct.
narrative.ai_impact: Likely uses include incident-report drafting, NERIS data integration, pre-incident planning, inspection and prevention documentation, risk analytics, scheduling, training administration, inventory, and alert triage. Suppression, rescue, emergency medicine, driving, maintenance, and scene command remain human and physical.
narrative.value_capture: Public agencies may convert productivity into readiness, service quality, avoided overtime, or budget savings, but those outcomes are not EBITDA retained through customer pricing. Commercial retention is therefore missing under the required lens.
narrative.firm_availability: Eligibility is observed at zero because 922160 is defined for government establishments and commercial firefighting belongs to 561990. Government reorganizations and procurement changes are not transferable-company control events.
narrative.demand_durability: Demand is durable and likely grows modestly because departments provide EMS, rescue, prevention, hazardous-material, disaster, and fire response. Incident mix, climate, demographics, budgets, prevention, and local service design widen the range.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The decisive limitation is classification, not operational quality. Additional uncertainties include absent l and n, incomplete incident reporting, local fiscal stress, volunteer capacity, interoperability, medical-data privacy, cybersecurity, false alerts, liability, and disaster volatility.
narrative.principal_driver: NERIS and department workflow modernization create a credible public-sector data and administrative opportunity.
narrative.principal_weakness: Official NAICS boundaries leave no transferable LMM firm or commercial value-capture mechanism inside 922160.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System United States, 2022
sources.supports: The manual defines 922160 as government firefighting and related fire protection, assigns commercial firefighting to 561990, forest firefighting to 115310, and ambulance-only services to 621910.

sources.title: Firefighters
sources.supports: BLS describes emergency response, suppression, rescue, medical care, reporting, maintenance, and drills, and projects firefighter employment to grow 3 percent from 2024 to 2034.

sources.title: About the National Emergency Response Information System
sources.supports: USFA describes a cloud-hosted all-hazards platform with near-real-time information, analytics, and integration of dispatch, GIS, sensor, biometric, and other data.

sources.title: National Fire Department Registry Summary, January 2024
sources.supports: USFA reports broad department service scope, including EMS at about 71 percent of departments, vehicle extrication at 78.5 percent, prevention and education at 64.9 percent, and wildfire or WUI service at 63.8 percent.

sources.title: 2024 U.S. Fire Administrator's Summit Emergency Medical Services Workgroup Report
sources.supports: USFA reports 31,867,167 NFIRS incidents in 2023, including 21,101,413 rescue and EMS calls, or 66.2 percent, and discusses funding and readiness pressure.

sources.title: National Interagency Coordination Center 2025 Annual Report
sources.supports: NIFC reports 77,850 wildfires and 5,131,474 acres burned in 2025, illustrating large annual workload and acreage volatility relevant to all-hazards planning.
